name: CyberArk Identity
abstract: Enterprise workforce IAM platform from Israel offering SSO, Adaptive MFA, lifecycle management, and privileged access. Native AD/LDAP sync via on-prem connector, SAML/OIDC/WS-Federation IdP, FIDO2 certified, FedRAMP High authorized. Includes built-in UBA/risk analytics, SCIM 2.0 provisioning, and a no-code workflow automation add-on. SaaS-only deployment on AWS across 13 global regions.

featureList.description: Social login supported with Google, Facebook, LinkedIn, and Microsoft as identity providers.
FIDO2 passkeys supported including platform authenticators (Windows Hello, Touch ID) and roaming authenticators (YubiKey). CyberArk is FIDO2 certified.
Email OTP supported as an authentication factor and can serve as a passwordless challenge, but is primarily positioned as an MFA factor rather than a dedicated standalone passwordless flow.
SMS OTP and phone call are supported as MFA factors; not specifically marketed as a primary passwordless authentication path.
No dedicated magic link authentication feature. Email OTP code is used instead of a click-to-login link.
No anonymous or guest authentication feature. CyberArk Identity requires authenticated user identity.

OATH TOTP supported via CyberArk Authenticator (desktop), CyberArk Identity mobile app (iOS/Android), or any OATH TOTP-compatible authenticator (Google Authenticator, Microsoft Authenticator). Hardware OTP tokens importable in bulk.
OATH HOTP (counter-based) supported alongside TOTP. Hardware HOTP tokens can be imported in bulk via CSV.
Physical FIDO U2F security keys (e.g. YubiKey) supported as a phishing-resistant MFA factor.
FIDO2/WebAuthn supported as MFA and passwordless factor, covering both platform authenticators and roaming security keys. CyberArk holds official FIDO2 certification.
Email confirmation code is a supported MFA challenge factor in authentication profiles.
SMS text message confirmation code and phone call OTP are both supported MFA factors.
No dedicated backup/recovery code feature. Account recovery is handled via self-service password reset using alternative MFA factors.
CyberArk Identity mobile app (iOS/Android) supports push notification MFA — users approve or deny login requests on their device. Wearables also supported as a token.
CyberArk Adaptive MFA evaluates AI/ML risk signals (device, network, location, user behavior, time of day) to dynamically step up or step down authentication requirements.
Cisco Duo is natively integrated as a configurable MFA factor. Administrators configure via Integration Key, Secret Key, and API hostname from the Duo Admin Panel.
Step-up authentication through adaptive MFA policies — additional factors are required based on resource sensitivity, risk score, or contextual changes mid-session.
Full SAML 2.0 IdP support for hundreds of certified app integrations. Supports IdP-initiated and SP-initiated SSO for B2B federation and enterprise application integration.
WS-Federation explicitly supported as an SSO IdP protocol. Used for Microsoft Azure Portal and other WS-Federation-aware applications.
OAuth 2.0 client credentials grant for M2M authentication. Applications exchange client ID and secret for access tokens to call protected APIs.
Federation with external IdPs via OIDC supported. Microsoft Entra ID can be federated using OIDC with PKCE as the recommended flow.
SAML 2.0 federation with external IdPs supported, including configurable routing rules and tenant-to-tenant SAML federation.

CyberArk Identity Connector (on-premises agent on a domain-joined Windows server) synchronizes AD users and groups to CyberArk Identity every 5 minutes. Supports multi-domain and multi-forest AD deployments.
Microsoft Entra ID (Azure AD) integration via SAML, OIDC, and SCIM. Users can be provisioned from Entra ID via inbound SCIM and Entra ID can act as an IdP for CyberArk.
Bulk import of up to 10,000 users from CSV or Excel (.xls/.xlsx) files via the Identity Administration portal wizard.
Direct password hash import is not supported. Credential migration uses Progressive Password Migration (PPM) which calls the legacy auth API at first login rather than importing pre-hashed passwords.
User data export available via the management REST API and reporting features. A dedicated bulk export UI is not explicitly documented.
Inbound SCIM provisioning handles bulk updates and deprovisioning from external systems. Direct bulk update/delete via CSV UI is not explicitly documented beyond initial import.
Not explicitly documented whether CSV imports perform create-or-update (upsert) vs. create-only semantics.
Users importable with custom attributes via CSV bulk import, supporting existing username schemes from legacy systems.
OATH HOTP/TOTP hardware tokens can be imported in bulk via CSV with seed values. Import of other MFA enrollment types (WebAuthn credentials, push app registrations) is not documented.
SCIM 2.0 inbound provisioning (CyberArk as SCIM server) accepts user and group creation, updates, and deletions from external systems such as Okta and Entra ID.
Outbound SCIM provisioning (CyberArk as SCIM client) pushes user accounts and attributes to SCIM-enabled target applications. Supports SCIM 1.1 and 2.0.
SCIM group objects supported both inbound and outbound, including inbound group sync from Entra ID/Okta and outbound role-to-group mapping to target apps.
JIT provisioning for application access is supported through SAML federation policies. First-time federated users are provisioned to the tenant on login.

Progressive Password Migration (PPM): users imported with 'TO_BE_MIGRATED' status have credentials validated against a configurable legacy auth API script on first login, then stored in CyberArk Cloud Directory without forcing a password reset.
User Portal self-service interface for managing profile, setting up MFA factors, resetting passwords, and configuring authentication.
Local account linking lets users authenticate as a target directory user based on matching attributes after authenticating against a source directory — useful for cross-directory migrations.
Administrators can disable or lock user accounts from the Identity Administration portal. Blocked users cannot authenticate until re-enabled.
Custom user attributes supported in CyberArk Cloud Directory, available in authentication scripts, OIDC claims, SAML assertions, and provisioning flows.
Application-level attributes and configuration exist but a distinct per-user-per-app attribute store (like Auth0's app_metadata) is not explicitly documented.
No specific documentation found for size limits on custom user attribute values.
User search available in the Identity Administration portal and via management REST APIs, across Cloud Directory and connected AD/LDAP directories.
Role-based access control is a core feature — roles control access to applications, policies, and administrative functions. Built-in predefined roles and custom roles are supported.

Sub-tenants (customers) can be created under a parent tenant for MSP use cases. Each sub-tenant has independent users, policies, and branding. B2B Identity supports external partner/vendor organizations.
Configurable password policies for CyberArk Cloud Directory: minimum length, complexity requirements, history, and expiration.
Login suffixes and tenant URLs are configurable. Up to multiple login suffixes per tenant can be defined to support different username formats.
Progressive profiling as a named feature (incrementally collecting user attributes over multiple sessions) is not documented in CyberArk Identity.
Attribute-based policies exist implicitly through adaptive MFA rules that evaluate contextual attributes (location, device, risk score, time of day). A formal named ABAC framework is not documented.
Fine-grained access control policies available — roles and policies control what users can access and under what authentication conditions, including per-application policy assignment.
OAuth 2.0 for API authorization with JWT access tokens. Custom OAuth 2.0 Server application type lets CyberArk Identity act as an authorization server for third-party APIs.
ISO 27001, ISO 27017 (cloud security), and ISO 27018 (PII in cloud) certified.
HIPAA Business Associate Agreement (BAA) available for customers processing PHI.

CyberArk Identity helps customers achieve PCI DSS compliance (MFA, access control, audit logging satisfy requirements) but product-level PCI DSS certification is not listed on CyberArk's own compliance page.
CSA STAR Certification held.
FedRAMP High Authorization achieved for Workforce Identity SaaS — one of the few IAM vendors with FedRAMP High (not just Moderate).
GDPR data portability supported — customer data can be retrieved via APIs upon request.
Right to erasure acknowledged in CyberArk's privacy notice. Individuals can request deletion of personal data under applicable conditions.
OAuth 2.0 consent screen presented during authorization. A full-featured end-user consent management platform is not a primary feature of the IAM product.
13 AWS regions: US East (Virginia), Canada (Montreal), South America (São Paulo), EU (Frankfurt, London, Milan), Middle East (UAE, Tel Aviv), APAC (Singapore, Sydney, Jakarta, Tokyo, Mumbai).
CyberArk Identity is SaaS-only. No self-hosted or on-premises deployment option for the Identity (SSO/MFA/lifecycle) product.
Audit logs available through the ISPSS Audit service covering all identity events. Specific default retention period is not publicly stated.
SIEM integration via TLS-encrypted syslog and REST API streaming. Documented integrations include Splunk, QRadar, and generic syslog receivers.
Security Insights section in the Identity Administration portal with dashboards for user logins, geographic login map, UBA alerts, and access events.
All data encrypted at rest using AWS KMS.
All communications enforced over TLS/HTTPS. SIEM log export also supports TLS-encrypted syslog.
BYOK via AWS KMS is documented for the ISPSS platform (Privilege Cloud). Whether BYOK specifically applies to the Identity SaaS service data tier requires confirmation with CyberArk sales.
CAPTCHA available to mitigate bot and brute-force attacks on login. Adaptive MFA also uses ML to detect automated attack patterns.
Account lockout policies protect against brute-force guessing. The server returns a consistent response whether the username is valid or invalid to prevent user enumeration. CAPTCHA available.
Adaptive MFA and UBA flag and step-up authentication for logins from suspicious IPs or new locations. Specific rate throttling of IPs as a standalone control is not explicitly documented.

Credential theft detection via the Threat Detection and Response product can alert on suspected credential compromise. Real-time breached-password check against a known-breach database at login is not documented as part of the core Identity product.
Credential Guard as a specific CyberArk Identity feature is not documented. Endpoint credential theft protection is available as a separate Endpoint Privilege Manager (EPM) capability.
No specific tenant ACL (IP allowlist/blocklist) feature is explicitly documented for CyberArk Identity beyond standard admin roles and policy controls.
User Behavior Analytics (UBA) uses device identity as a risk signal — unknown or new devices elevate risk scores. Device enrollment and trust policies also rely on device fingerprinting.
Sub-tenant administrators can configure their own branding (logo, name, colors, login page) independently from the parent tenant. MSPs can manage sub-tenants with separate branding.
Each tenant and sub-tenant has independent authentication profiles and MFA policies with separate MFA requirements from the parent tenant.
CyberArk hosts the login/user portal at a tenant URL (e.g., company.cyberark.com). Customizable with brand name, logo, and sign-in messages.
Embeddable authentication widgets and JavaScript SDK allow apps to embed authentication flows without redirecting to CyberArk's hosted page.
Full white-label supported: CyberArk branding can be replaced with a custom brand name and logo throughout the Identity Administration portal and User Portal.
Limited language support: English and Japanese (full portal), Czech, Polish, and Romanian (User Portal, mobile app, auth widget, email templates). Not a comprehensive global localization suite.
Pre-built widgets, JavaScript SDK, iOS SDK, and Android SDK provide ready-to-use UI components for embedding authentication and self-service flows.
Built-in dashboards: User Logins dashboard (date/time/user/result), User Login Map (geographic locations), and Overview dashboard. UBA provides behavioral analytics on top.
Official SDKs for JavaScript (browser/SPA), iOS (Swift), Android (Kotlin/Java), and Java (server-side M2M). REST APIs available for all operations.
Comprehensive REST management API for users, roles, applications, policies, authentication configuration, and provisioning.
Rate limits exist (Policy APIs capped at 30 calls/minute per category). Full authentication API rate limit documentation is not publicly detailed.
Custom JavaScript scripts configurable in the admin portal for OIDC claims customization, provisioning scripts, and Progressive Password Migration logic.
Only JavaScript is documented for custom scripts and hooks. TypeScript support is not mentioned.
Custom domains via CNAME with SSL certificate upload supported, up to 10 custom domains per tenant.
No deploy CLI tool documented for CyberArk Identity. Terraform provider is the infrastructure-as-code option.
CyberArk Identity Security Terraform Provider available in the Terraform public registry for managing CyberArk Identity configurations as IaC.
Progressive Password Migration uses a configurable JavaScript hook to call a custom legacy authentication API, acting as a delegated credential source for migration.
Webhooks supported for triggering external web services on security alerts and UBA events. Payload format uses FreeMarker templating with dynamic CyberArk Identity UBA variables.
Login page fully customizable: brand name, logo, colors, sign-in message, and custom domain. White-label replaces all CyberArk references.
Email template content and styling are customizable. Configuring a custom SMTP relay or external email provider is not explicitly documented.
Email templates for sign-in, enrollment, and notifications are customizable via Settings > Customization > Account Customization. Logo, wording, and styling can be modified.
Custom OIDC/JWT claims configurable via a script editor in the OAuth2 Server application. Supports adding non-default standard claims and fully custom claims.
CyberArk Identity Flows — a no-code visual automation editor with drag-and-drop logic and 700+ app connectors for building identity lifecycle and event-driven automation workflows.
Identity verification available via Transmit Security integration within CyberArk Flows workflows. Not a native built-in feature.
No support for DIDs (Decentralized Identifiers), verifiable credentials, or Web3 identity standards.
No built-in billing or subscription management integration.

CyberArk has publicly committed to agentic AI identity governance — automating lifecycle management of AI agent identities. As of mid-2026, this is an emerging roadmap initiative rather than a GA feature.
